title: SEGMENT 15 — The ancestry problem in pharmacogenomics

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — The ancestry problem in pharmacogenomics
I've alluded to this several times, and now I want to confront it directly.
Pharmacogenomics has a serious population genetics problem, and it is one of the deepest equity issues in modern medicine.
Here's the situation.

The variants we test for in clinical pharmacogenomics, and the guidelines that translate variants into recommended actions, were largely derived from studies conducted in European populations — predominantly White Europeans and European-descended Americans.

This is a consequence of who gets enrolled in clinical research, where the major research institutions have historically been located, and which populations have been the focus of large-scale genomic studies.
The consequence is twofold, and both consequences are damaging.
Consequence one.
Allele frequencies vary dramatically by ancestry.
The CYP2D6\4 allele that's common in Europeans is rare in East Asians.
The CYP2D6\10 allele that's common in East Asians is rare in Europeans.
The HLA-B\*1502 allele that causes Stevens-Johnson syndrome with carbamazepine is rare in Europeans and common in many Asian populations.
The CYP2B6 variants that affect efavirenz metabolism are particularly common in African populations.
The CYP2C19 variants that determine clopidogrel response are very different in frequency between European, Asian, and African populations.
So a test panel calibrated for European patients — covering the variants most common in Europeans — will miss the most clinically-relevant variants in patients of other ancestries.
If you test an East Asian patient for CYP2D6 and only look for the European variants, you'll miss the CYP2D6\*10 allele and underestimate the prevalence of intermediate metabolizers in that patient.
If you test an African patient for CYP2D6 and only look for the European variants, you'll miss several common African variants entirely.
Consequence two.
The clinical guidelines may not be validated in non-European populations.

Even when we know the variants, we often don't have large enough studies in non-European populations to know how the variants behave clinically — whether the dose adjustments derived from European data are correct for other groups, whether there are interactions with other variants that are common in one population but not another, whether the metabolizer phenotype categories translate cleanly across ancestries.
There are some specific failures that have caused real harm.

The classic carbamazepine SJS/TEN story — where the most important HLA association for the world's most populous countries was missed for decades because the early research was done in Europeans — is one.

The classic CYP2C9 warfarin story is another: the dosing algorithms derived from European patients perform poorly in African American patients, partly because additional CYP2C9 variants that are common in African ancestry but rare in European ancestry weren't included in early panels.
The field has been making progress, but slowly.
All of Us is deliberately oversampling non-European populations.
The H3Africa consortium is building African genomics infrastructure.
Several Asian countries have built national pharmacogenomics databases focused on locally-relevant variants.
CPIC guidelines now explicitly call out where the evidence base is thin for particular populations, and recommend testing for population-relevant variants.
Major commercial pharmacogenomics panels increasingly include the major non-European variants.
But the legacy effect is real.
For decades, the pharmacogenomics infrastructure was built around European patients, and the rest of the world has been catching up.
If you're a clinician serving a non-European population — or, more realistically, any population that's not exclusively European — you have to be aware of this.
The default panels and the default guidelines may have gaps for your patients.
The right answer is to use comprehensive panels that include population-relevant variants, and to be aware of ancestry-specific guidance where it exists.
This is the ancestry problem.

It's not unique to pharmacogenomics — it pervades all of medical genetics — but it bites particularly hard here, because the consequences are immediate, concrete, and measured in adverse drug reactions and treatment failures.
A drug that's safe and effective in a European patient may be neither in an Asian or African patient, for reasons that come down to allele frequencies the early studies didn't capture.

The solution is straightforward to state and slow to implement: build the evidence base across all populations, design panels to detect the variants that matter in each population, and update the guidelines as the evidence accumulates.
The field is doing this.
It's not done.
